skill.name: routeros-sniffer
skill.description: RouterOS packet capture and TZSP streaming for protocol debugging. Use when: capturing packets on RouterOS, setting up /tool/sniffer, streaming live traffic via TZSP, using firewall mangle action=sniff-tzsp, debugging network protocols on MikroTik, receiving TZSP with Wireshark or tshark, saving pcap files from RouterOS, or when the user mentions packet sniffer, TZSP, sniff-tzsp, /tool/sniffer, or packet capture on RouterOS.
skill.summary: Capture packets on RouterOS devices and stream to Wireshark or save as pcap files using TZSP protocol.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: This is a pure documentation skill containing markdown files that explain RouterOS packet capture procedures. All 154 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES: the scanner incorrectly flagged backticks in code examples as Ruby execution, sudo commands as privilege escalation, and relative paths as path traversal. No executable code exists in this skill. The content teaches legitimate network diagnostic procedures using standard tools like Wireshark and tshark.

content.user_title: Capture RouterOS packets with TZSP streaming
content.value_statement: Network engineers need to debug protocols on MikroTik routers but lack standard tools like tcpdump. This skill provides complete workflows for capturing packets using RouterOS built-in sniffer and mirroring traffic via TZSP to Wireshark for analysis.
actual_capabilities: Configure /tool/sniffer for full interface capture with file output or TZSP streaming
Set up firewall mangle rules with action=sniff-tzsp for targeted traffic mirroring
Receive and decode TZSP packets using Wireshark or tshark on the host machine
Save captured packets to pcap files on RouterOS flash storage
Debug network protocols using QEMU CHR instances without physical hardware
Filter captured traffic by IP address, port, protocol, or interface
limitations: Hardware-offloaded bridge traffic and wireless unicast are not visible to the sniffer
Packet capture on physical hardware requires network tap or port mirroring setup
Free CHR license has a 1 Mbps speed limit which may affect high-speed captures
RouterOS must be version 7.20 or later for PCAPNG file format support

use_cases.title: Debugging VPN connectivity issues on RouterOS
use_cases.description: Network administrators can capture VPN handshake packets to diagnose why tunnels fail to establish or drop unexpectedly.

use_cases.title: Analyzing firewall rule effectiveness
use_cases.description: Use sniff-tzsp to mirror traffic matching specific firewall rules, then verify in Wireshark that packets are being processed as expected.

use_cases.title: Protocol development testing for RouterOS automation
use_cases.description: Capture and inspect API requests and responses to understand RouterOS REST API behavior during script development.

prompt_templates.title: Start basic packet capture
prompt_templates.prompt: Start capturing all traffic on ether1 and save it to a pcap file called debug.pcap on the router.

prompt_templates.title: Mirror specific traffic to Wireshark
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up TZSP streaming so all DNS traffic is mirrored to my workstation at 192.168.1.100 for analysis in Wireshark.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a specific host connection
prompt_templates.prompt: Mirror all traffic to and from host 10.10.10.50 to help debug why it cannot reach the internet.

prompt_templates.title: Capture with CHR for testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to set up a packet capture lab using QEMU CHR and tshark to test network scripts before deploying to production.

output_examples.input: Start capturing ICMP traffic on ether1 and stream to Wireshark
output_examples.output:
Configuration commands:

/tool/sniffer/set streaming-enabled=yes streaming-server=192.168.1.50:37008 filter-interface=ether1 filter-ip-protocol=icmp
/tool/sniffer/start

On your workstation, run:
tshark -i any -f "udp port 37008"

Stop capture with:
/tool/sniffer/stop

output_examples.input: Save packets from the last 5 minutes to a file
output_examples.output:
Save memory buffer:
/tool/sniffer/save file-name=/flash/capture.pcap

Download via SCP:
scp admin@192.168.88.1:/flash/capture.pcap .

best_practices: Always use filter-stream=yes (default) to avoid capturing your own TZSP packets and creating feedback loops
Clean up sniffer config and mangle rules after debugging to prevent unintended traffic mirroring
Use targeted mangle rules instead of full interface capture when you only need specific traffic flows
anti_patterns: Do not capture without filters on high-bandwidth interfaces as this can overwhelm the router and fill storage
Avoid using filter-stream=no unless you specifically need to capture the TZSP stream itself
Do not leave streaming-server configured when not in use as it will continue sending packets to the receiver

faq.question: Why am I not seeing any packets in my capture?
faq.answer: Check if traffic is hardware-offloaded (bridges, switches) or wireless client-to-client. Use /tool/sniffer/quick for interactive verification. Ensure filter-stream=yes to avoid TZSP feedback loops.

faq.question: Can I capture packets on a remote RouterOS device from my laptop?
faq.answer: Yes, configure streaming-server to your laptop IP and run tshark or Wireshark listening on UDP port 37008. Ensure your firewall allows incoming UDP on that port.

faq.question: What is the difference between /tool/sniffer and firewall mangle sniff-tzsp?
faq.answer: /tool/sniffer captures all traffic on specified interfaces. Mangle sniff-tzsp mirrors only packets matching firewall rules for surgical, per-flow mirroring.

faq.question: How long are packets kept in the sniffer memory buffer?
faq.answer: Packets expire after 10 minutes by default. Use /tool/sniffer/save to persist captures or configure file output for continuous recording.

faq.question: Why is my CHR capture so slow?
faq.answer: The free CHR license has a 1 Mbps speed limit. This is a licensing restriction, not a sniffer issue. Upgrade to a paid CHR license for higher speeds.

faq.question: Can I use this to capture encrypted traffic like HTTPS?
faq.answer: You can capture the packets but not decrypt them without the TLS keys. The skill captures at the network layer only. Encrypted content remains encrypted in the capture.
